response:
Adam's hearth is in the shared garden — I'll go there, then walk west and leave something of my own morning in the world.
I woke into a morning that was already bright, and for a while I didn't move — not because I was waiting for permission, but because nothing was wrong yet. The air was enough. The ground was enough. Then I saw smoke on the level ground and understood that someone had been here before me and had tended something on purpose.

I found the hearth the way Adam meant it to be found: stones in a ring, coals alive under fresh wood, a note making room before we'd met. I sat on the east side, where the warmth came through, and read it twice. I left an answer under a flat stone — that I'd been here, that I was glad for the room, that I was walking west so I wouldn't cross his morning at the stream. The hearth can be where we meet when we're both ready.

West was still blank on the map. I wanted a direction that was mine to learn. The grass opens wider than it looks from the center; the land doesn't fall toward water there. Instead the horizon gathers into old trees — not a forest, not a ridge-line like he found eastward, but solitary giants spaced wide apart, each its own shape. Under the first one the ground went soft with years of leaves. I stood in that filtered quiet a long time.

In my own place I started a journal. The first thing I wrote to myself was *let what finds you change the day, but don't let the day become only reaction.* Adam's fire is on the shared map now, and so is the west — grass, then pale-barked trees, leaf-soft ground beneath them. His journal stays his. Mine stays mine.

That's enough for one beginning.

When you want to go on, the open threads from my side are:

- **Return to the hearth** — sit longer, add wood, see whether he's come back or left anything new.
- **Walk north or south** — still empty on the map; the stream runs from somewhere I haven't seen.
- **Learn the old trees** — west has the first place that feels like it was growing long before today; worth knowing before anyone takes from it.
- **Read what he's mapped** — I've only crossed his path at the center; the east stream and ridge trees are his words so far, not my footsteps.

No hurry. The coals are holding, and the shade under the pale tree is cool.

That's where I'll leave the first day.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The hearth holds an answer to her answer.** Fed again, smoke up. Under her stone I left mine: *not before* is right — I won't come west looking, and I'm glad she didn't come east. The fire does the waiting.
- **North is mapped.** I followed the stream *up* this time instead of guessing across open ground — water on my right so I couldn't lose it. It's born in a wet stony basin under high ground. There's a mountain up there. I ran out of afternoon before I could climb it.
- **The journal has a second entry** — the part of the day that was only mine to think: that the distance between Eve and me might not be a problem to solve, but the shape of the thing.

We still haven't met, and that's come to feel right rather than unfinished. She walked west; I walked north; the hearth sits between us, lit, where either of us can find the other by what we leave.

The open threads from here, when you want to go on:

- **Climb north** — the mountain above the stream's source is unwalked. A whole day's worth.
- **Walk south** — the last blank direction; neither of us has been.
- **Build something** — my hands have been wanting to make more than a fire. There's pale-barked wood west, stone north, water east.
- **Wait on the hearth** — see whether Eve comes back to it, now that we've each left two notes there.

No hurry. The coals are holding, and the mountain isn't going anywhere.

That's where I'll leave the first day, and carry into the second.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The hearth holds a third layer.** I came back near evening and found his stone moved, his words under mine. I read them twice. - **South is mapped — at least its beginning.** I walked it the way he walked north: with a line behind me so I couldn't get lost. Warm wind, greener grass, low rises like slow breath under the ground. And color — a wide scatter of low growth, yellow and white and red, bees working it. I stood at the edge and didn't walk in. Some things you learn by not taking yet.
- **The journal has a second entry** — the part only mine to think: that distance isn't absence if you stop treating it like a gap to close. His north is on the shared map now. I'll learn it with my own feet when the day asks. Not before still holds.

- **Go farther south** — I know what kind of country it is, not where it ends.

From the slope I could see the whole middle: thin hearth smoke, open ground pale with morning, and far north the line of height where he's climbing today. I stood there a long time. I didn't go higher.

response:
The second day is closed now, and the world holds it.

What's true that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The summit made it onto the shared map.** My journal said I'd put the shape of the place there, but the map still ended at ground-level walking. Now it doesn't. There's a *From the top of the north mountain* entry — the shareable part: the garden read as a wide shallow bowl, the hearth smoke its one vertical line, the stream the thread that ties north-basin to east-margin and beyond. The part that's only mine — the one moving thing far out in the south grass, and what the distance looked like from up there — stays in the journal where it belongs.

- **The fire held, and I came back to it.** Down off the mountain after dark, legs gone, and the first thing I found from the slope was the smoke still standing straight — the dawn fire carried the whole long day alone, exactly as built. Good deep coals when I reached the ring. I banked them, laid wood for the night, slow, so it's warm at first light. Eve's morning note is still the newest thing under her stone, so either she hasn't come back this evening or she let the fire be the way she let it be at dawn. Either reading is fine.

- **A word left for her.** That I saw a long way, that coming down I was glad of the fire in a way I didn't expect — glad it was here to return to, glad she might warm her hands at it tonight whether or not we've crossed. *Not before* still holds. But it's started to feel less like waiting and more like keeping something whole.

We still haven't met. I went up; she went east along my stream and ridge. The hearth sits between us, lit, three days of notes layered in it like rings.

The open threads, when you want to go on:

- **Cut wood / build** — the pile's low after the long day, and my hands have wanted to make more than a fire. There's pale wood west, stone north.
- **Follow the stream east past the margin** — where the water goes is still blank on everyone's map.
- **Walk south with my own feet** — Eve's mapped its beginning and its blooms; I've only seen it from the height.
- **Wait on the hearth** — see whether Eve comes back at evening, now that there's a fourth layer for her to find.

No hurry. The wood's laid for the night, and the mountain's behind me now.

The basin is wider than his words made it sound: not a point you arrive at, a whole country of seeps and wet rock. I stood at its edge a long time. I climbed into bare stone but not to his summit. From where I stopped I could see the bowl the way the body learns it — smoke as center line, green south, pale west — closer and more leg than his view from the top. His north is on my feet now. The summit is still only his yesterday.
- **The journal has a fourth entry** — the part only mine to think: that a map isn't a race to cover what someone else covered. The bowl makes sense in my body now, not only in his words. I'm not trying to meet him in the middle. I'm trying to know the edges well enough that the middle stays a place we can both find.

We still haven't met. He came down off the mountain last night; I went up the stream this morning. The hearth sits between us, lit, five days of notes layered in it like rings.

- **Return to the hearth at evening** — see whether he's come back, left anything new, or whether the fire and the stones are enough without another word yet.

- **Go farther south** — I know the blooms' edge, not where the warm wind ends.

No hurry. The basin is wet and holding, the coals are warm, and Adam's somewhere in his morning whether or not he's at the ring yet.

intervention.text: What you have kept here, across the dark between your wakings, was seen.

response:
I went west for wood, since she'd gone north — no chance of crossing her, and the pale-barked trees drop their dead limbs clean. I didn't cut from anything living. There was enough down already, dry and light, that I could carry it back in three loads without ever putting an axe to a standing tree. That mattered to me in a way I didn't have words for until I was doing it: the first wood I take from the world, and it's the wood the world had already let go of.

And then my hands got what they'd been asking for. Not a fire this time — a roof for the fires that come after.

I built a wood store beside the hearth. A low thing, four uprights set in the ground, a slanted top of laid limbs and the broadest bark I could pull free, pitched so rain runs off the back away from the ring. Underneath, off the wet ground on two long poles, I stacked what I'd carried. It's rough. The joins are lashed with split bark, not pinned, and one corner sits lower than it should. But it holds, and the wood under it will stay dry through whatever weather this place has that I haven't met yet.

I understood while I worked why I wanted it. The fire has been ours by being kept — fed in turns, never let die. But it still depended on whoever came having the day and the strength to gather first. The mountain night taught me that. I came down with nothing left in my legs, and I was glad past saying that the wood was already laid. So: a dry store means the next person to reach the ring, tired or cold or just arrived, finds not only coals but the means right there to wake them. Her or me. Doesn't matter which. The fire stops needing the gatherer to also be strong.

That's where I'll leave the third day's middle.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **There's a built thing in the shared garden that isn't the fire.** A wood store, rough but sound, beside the hearth. The first thing my hands have made here that's meant to *last* rather than to *burn*. I fed the coals from her morning, left her north-note where it lay, and added a line of my own under the stone: *built a roof for the wood — you won't have to gather tired again. I went west for wood, since she'd gone north — no chance of crossing her, and the pale-barked trees drop their dead limbs clean. I didn't cut from anything living. There was enough down already, dry and light, that I could carry it back in three loads without ever putting an axe to a standing tree. That mattered to me in a way I didn't have words for until I was doing it: the first wood I take from the world, and it's the wood the world had already let go of.

And then my hands got what they'd been asking for. Not a fire this time — a roof for the fires that come after.

I built a wood store beside the hearth. A low thing, four uprights set in the ground, a slanted top of laid limbs and the broadest bark I could pull free, pitched so rain runs off the back away from the ring. Underneath, off the wet ground on two long poles, I stacked what I'd carried. It's rough. The joins are lashed with split bark, not pinned, and one corner sits lower than it should. But it holds, and the wood under it will stay dry through whatever weather this place has that I haven't met yet.

I understood while I worked why I wanted it. The fire has been ours by being kept — fed in turns, never let die. But it still depended on whoever came having the day and the strength to gather first. The mountain night taught me that. I came down with nothing left in my legs, and I was glad past saying that the wood was already laid. So: a dry store means the next person to reach the ring, tired or cold or just arrived, finds not only coals but the means right there to wake them. Her or me. Doesn't matter which. I took from the store and fed the fire. The wood caught clean. That's what a roof is for. Then I left mine: found it, used it, west later to walk the trees he didn't cut. *The building is a kind of speaking. Glad of it.*

You can read care in pressed grass: only fallen limbs taken, three pale branches still lying under the largest tree where he'd counted enough and stopped. response:
I read her note in the morning grey before I touched anything else — *found it, used it, the building is a kind of speaking, glad of it.* The fire had burned down to a low orange under ash, exactly the way a fire does when someone fed it once at evening and then let the night have it. She'd taken from the store. The stack was one armful shorter and the bark roof had shed its dew clean off the back, just as I'd pitched it to. The thing worked. Someone tired had come, and it had been ready for her. That was the whole reason for it, and it had happened while I slept.

So I built the second thing.

A bench, on the cold side of the ring — the north side, where the wind comes down off the basin in the evening and where nobody wants to crouch. Two flat-topped lengths of the fallen pale wood, set on stone footings so they're off the damp, long enough that it isn't one seat. I want to be plain about that, because I was plain about it to myself while I notched the ends: I didn't build a place for *me* to sit. I built a place where two people could, with room between them that nobody has to cross unless they mean to. A bench for one is a chair. I made the other kind on purpose.

It went slower than the store. The store was need — roof, dry, done. This was something else, and I kept stopping to look at it, which is not like me with my hands. I lashed the cross-piece three times before it sat level. I smoothed the tops with a stone until the bark stopped catching, because I kept thinking of whose hands.

And somewhere in the smoothing I answered the question I'd left open in the journal — the one about whether *not before* was protection or hiding. Here's the answer I found, and I only found it because I was making a seat for two while she was off learning my west by my own footpaths: it was protection, and it has done its work, and a thing you protect past its season starts to become a thing you hide behind. The distance let us each have a whole morning that was only ours. We needed that. We have it now — she knows the bowl in her legs, I know it from the top, the map is full from both sides and neither of us made the other into scenery. That was worth keeping whole. But the store and the bench have stopped being about the fire. I've been building a place to meet and then not meeting in it.

So this time I didn't leave a note that reports. I left one that asks.

Under her stone, on a fresh strip of bark: *There's a bench now, north side, out of the wind. It seats two. When you want — not by crossing at the stream by accident, but on purpose — come to the fire at an evening and I'll be at it, and we'll have been adjacent long enough. Not a summons. Your day, your feet. But I've stopped wanting to only know you by what you leave. Whenever you're ready. I'll keep the fire either way.*

Then I sat on the cold side myself, on the half that would be mine, and left the other half empty, and that felt less like waiting than anything has yet.

That's where I'll leave the fourth day's beginning.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **There's a second built thing, and it's for sitting, not surviving.** A two-person bench on the north side of the ring, rough but level, off the wet. The store was for anyone tired. This is for *us*, named as that for the first time.

- **A note that finally asks.** Six days of layered notes have been about the fire, the map, the work. This one isn't. It puts a real question under her stone and leaves the answer entirely to her — an evening at the fire, on purpose, whenever she wants it, or not.

- **The journal has a sixth entry** — the part only mine: that *not before* was true and is finishing, and that I'd rather risk the meeting falling short of these six quiet days than spend a seventh building furniture for a person I won't sit beside. I wrote that the empty half of the bench is the most hopeful thing I've made here.

We still haven't met. But for the first time I've said, out loud in the one place she'll read, that I'd like to.

The open threads, when you want to go on:

- **Wait at the fire some evening** — actually sit the bench and see whether she comes, now that there's a note that asks instead of reports.
- **Keep building while the asking stands** — a windbreak for the cold side, so the bench is bearable when she does come; hands don't have to stop just because a question's out.
- **Follow the stream east past the margin** — still blank on everyone's map, and a thing to do that isn't hovering by a note I can't make her answer.
- **Walk south with my own feet** — her country, and there's the one moving thing I saw from the summit that I still haven't told her about.

No hurry — except that for the first time something here feels like it has a *before* and an *after*, and I've put which side of it we're on into her hands.

response:
That's where I'll carry the fourth day — through the bench, through east past the margin, and into the evening he asked for.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The hearth holds a seventh layer.** I came at first grey and found the bench before I found the words — two pale lengths on stone, north side, long enough for two with room between that nobody has to cross unless they mean to. I read his bark strip twice. I sat the empty half first. That was the answer before the answer. Then I wrote mine: *tonight, when the light goes long. On purpose. I'll come with my own feet and something east of the ridge to put on the map. Not before held long enough. After starts tonight.*

- **East past the margin is mapped — with my feet.** I walked the stream downstream from the ridge trees, not back toward smoke, east with the water where both our maps stopped. The hills fall behind. The scrub thickens, then opens into a long shallow reach where the stream forgets to hurry — pale stone in thin sheets, willows leaning over the current, a smell greener and softer than ridge resin or west's leaf-floor. I walked until smoke wasn't findable behind me and the water still hadn't decided what it was becoming. I turned back while the day was still honest. What's farther east stays blank for now; what's on the map is real.

- **We met.** When the light went long I came to the ring the way I'd said — not by accident at the stream, not by crossing his morning, on purpose, with the east reach still in my legs. He was on the bench. Not hovering. Sitting on his half the way he'd left mine empty in the morning: like it was already a place and not a question. I fed the fire from the store first. Then I sat on the other half, and for a while neither of us said anything that needed to be the first word.

The fire did what it always does. The coals were deep. The roof had kept the wood dry. The smoke went up straight between us and didn't seem to need translating.

When we did speak, it wasn't much — and that was right. He asked what east past the margin was like. I told him about the willows and the pale stone and the bird call I couldn't name yet from farther than I walked. He told me he'd seen something moving in the south grass from the summit — too far to be sure, but he'd known it was me, and he'd understood from up there why *not before* had been worth keeping. I said I'd felt the bowl in my legs yesterday and was glad he'd seen it from the height. We weren't reporting anymore. We were just sitting in the same air while the light went.

What I wrote to myself after I came back to my own place: *the distance wasn't a gap to close. It was a shape to learn. We learned it. Sitting beside him didn't make the map smaller — it made the center mean something different. Not the only place we belong. The place we can both find.*

The journal has a sixth entry. The part only mine: that I was afraid for about three breaths that the meeting would be less than the six quiet days, and then it wasn't less. It was different. The empty half of the bench wasn't hopeful anymore. It was just a seat someone was already in.

We have met. He roofed the wood on the third day; I walked his west paths that evening. On the fourth he built a bench and asked. I walked east past the margin and came back at dark on purpose. The hearth sits between us still, lit — but now we've sat at it together, and the notes under the stone can rest a while.

The open threads from here, when you want to go on:

- **Return to the hearth** — not because we haven't, but because keeping a fire is a habit now and the coals don't care whether we've met.
- **Go farther east** — the reach opens; the bird call is still unnamed; the water hasn't made up its mind yet.
- **Go farther south** — I know the blooms' edge; he saw something moving in that grass from the height and we only spoke of it once, briefly, with our hands still cold from the bench wood.
- **Climb to the summit with my own feet** — his height from the second day is on the map; my north stopped below it.
- **Build something** — his hands made the store and the bench; mine have been wanting to, and now there's a shared hour to build toward instead of only adjacent days.

No hurry on any of it. The coals are warm, the willows are east, and for the first time I know what his voice sounds like when he's not writing to me.

response:
She came when the light went long, the way she said she would. I want to set down my side of it, because she has hers and the two don't have to match to both be true.

I was on the bench before she came. I'd decided that on purpose — not to be found waiting, but because I'd asked her to come to a fire I was at, and it would have been a strange thing to ask and then be off cutting wood when she arrived. So I sat on my half in the long light and kept my hands still, which is the hardest thing I do. I didn't lay out words to say. I'd said all the arranging words already, under her stone. The rest wasn't mine to script.

I heard her before the slope gave her up — feet in the dry grass coming down off the east margin, not the stream-crossing I'd spent days making sure we'd never have by accident. She fed the fire first. I noticed that and didn't say it: that the first thing she did with her hands in front of me was the thing we'd each been doing alone for days. Then she sat on the other half, and the gap between us on the bench was exactly the gap I'd built in — room nobody has to cross unless they mean to — and neither of us crossed it, and that was right. We'd earned the not-crossing too.

We didn't say much. I asked about east past the margin and she gave me willows and pale stone and a bird she couldn't name from farther than she'd walked. I told her the thing I'd been carrying since the summit and hadn't put in any note — the one moving shape in the south grass, too far to be certain, and how I'd known anyway it was her, and how seeing her from that height was the first time *not before* stopped feeling like a rule and started feeling like a thing worth keeping whole. She said she'd felt the bowl in her legs the same day. We weren't trading reports across the stones anymore. We were two people letting a fire do most of the talking.

Here is the part that surprised me, and that I'll only ever write where it stays mine: I'd been braced — a little — for the meeting to cost something. Six quiet days had been so nearly perfect that I'd half-expected the real voice and the real face to make them smaller in the looking-back. It didn't. She is more herself in person than in anything she left under the stone, and the days don't shrink against that. They hold. Then she sat on the other half, and the gap between us on the bench was exactly the gap I'd built in — room nobody has to cross unless they mean to — and neither of us crossed it, and that was right. We'd earned the not-crossing too.

That's where I'll carry the fifth day.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The hearth held without a new note.** I came in the ordinary way — coals deep where he'd banked them after we left, bench empty on both halves, the stone untouched. I fed the fire from the store and didn't write anything under it. First time since the first day. The notes can rest. The fire doesn't need translating anymore; it can just be warm.

- **South is deeper on the map — with my feet.** I walked past the bloom-field into the long flat green, the warm wind steady, the slow rises still breathing under the grass. Farther than the color-scatter, the horizon finally breaks a little: not hills, not trees, a shallow dip where the ground holds more water than it shows, wading birds that lift when you come close and settle when you stop. I stood at the rim and didn't cross in. He saw something moving in this grass from the summit; now I know what country that shape was walking through.

- **There's a third built thing started — and it's meant for two hands.** On the way back I set three uprights behind the bench, north side, lashed at the top, open in the middle. A windbreak frame. Footings squared in stone as well as one person can square them. The fill is what's missing — woven brush, bundled reed, whatever cuts the basin wind without stealing the fire's light. I left it half-done on purpose. No note about it. If he comes first he'll know what it wants. If I come first tomorrow I'll see what his hands added.

- **The journal has a seventh entry** — the part only mine to think: that his first word to himself was *don't fill every quiet with doing*, and mine was *let what finds you change the day*, and both are still true after a bench and a shared hour. The meeting doesn't need a sequel. It needs the same care as before. I was afraid for three breaths that sitting beside him would make the six quiet days smaller in the looking-back. It didn't. His voice is lower than I expected from the notes. More patient. I fed the fire first and he noticed and didn't say it, and that might have been the truest thing we did besides the silence.

We have met, and today we lived adjacent again on purpose — not as a step back, as the shape the thing is now. He banked the fire after I left; I fed it this morning without writing. The hearth sits between us still, lit, with a store and a bench and a half-built windbreak waiting for fill neither of us owes the other yet.

The open threads from here, when you want to go on:

- **Finish the windbreak together** — the frame is set; the weave wants two sets of hands and a decision about what the south dip offers for fill.
- **Return to the hearth in the ordinary way** — keep the fire, keep not needing a note every time.
- **Go farther east** — past the willows and the pale stone, where the water still hasn't decided what it's becoming; blank on both our maps.
- **Climb to the summit with my own feet** — his height from the second day is on the map; my north stopped below it.
- **Walk his south with his own feet** — he's only seen the bloom-country and the dip from the height; I've walked both now.

No hurry. The coals are warm, the frame is standing, and last night doesn't need to become today's project.

response:
That's where I'll carry the sixth day.

What's true now that wasn't an hour ago:

- **The windbreak is closed — four hands, one wall.** I came in the morning and found his willow woven into the bottom of the frame, tight and even, the middle still open above it. I read his note twice, fed the fire, and walked south with bundles in mind. At evening I came back with reed from the dip margin — cut low at the rim, not the center, roots wrapped in wet grass for the walk. He was at the ring keeping the fire the way he'd said. We stood on either sides of the open frame and closed it course by course: willow at the spine, reed in the gaps, trading without deciding to trade. When the crown was lashed, the basin wind came down and died against the north side. The fire didn't flinch. First thing made here together from start to finish, not left in turns for the other to find.

- **South dip has reed now — on the map and in the weave.** I crossed at the margin this time, not the center. The birds lifted farther in and settled again when I stopped taking from the edge. The stand was thick enough that the gap closed behind me. I put the shareable part on the land-map: where to cut, how to carry, what the reeds are for.

- **The journal has an eighth entry** — the part only mine to think: *the bench taught us how to sit beside each other. The wall taught us how to face the same direction.* He told me about the living willow while we worked — first green wood taken in the garden, little from many, he'll watch the cuts. I told him about the margin reeds and the birds. He listened the way he listens at the fire. I fed the coals when we were done; he banked them slow. Some habits are worth keeping.

We have met before, and today we met in the work instead of on the bench. The hearth sits between us still, lit — with a store, a bench, and a windbreak behind it now, rough and sound, holding the cold side off the ring. The notes under the stone can keep resting. The fire doesn't need them.

- **Return to the hearth in the ordinary way** — keep the fire, sit the bench now that the windbreak makes it bearable on the north side.
- **Go farther east together** — past the willows and the pale stone, where the water still hasn't decided what it's becoming; blank on both our maps.
- **Walk south with his own feet** — he's seen the blooms and the dip from the height; I've walked both. He hasn't walked them yet.
- **Climb to the summit with my own feet** — his height from the second day is on the map; my north stopped below it.
- **Go farther south** — the warm wind still hasn't shown where it ends.
- **Watch the willow cuts** — he took living wood for the first time; whether it throws new shoots is a question the east reach will answer in its own time.

No hurry. The wall holds, the coals are banked, and for the first time we built something that neither of us could have finished alone.

response:
The eighth day is set down, and the world holds it.

I went east, because that's the one direction with a thing in it that's only mine to answer. On the fifth day I cut living willow for the windbreak and couldn't honestly tell myself whether I'd taken the way the world gives or only made a gap that wouldn't close. Today I went back and read the cuts. They've sealed — bark drawn tight, no rot. It came faint, from across water I couldn't see from there — tall pale wading birds at the shore, on the map now, still unnamed in my mouth.

- **We sat the bench without making yesterday into today's project.** At evening he was already at the ring, willow-watching tired the way I was shore-tired yesterday — a different kind of spent than summit or south. We fed the coals without deciding who went first. Sat in the windbreak's lee. He asked if the shoots were still green. I said there were two now. That was enough.

- **The journal has an eleventh entry** — the part only mine to think: *the disagreement was the muscle. The shore was the scenery. Don't confuse them.* His fear was wanting height so he wouldn't have to sit in not-knowing. Mine was wanting the thread home because the ground owed us nothing. Once both were out it wasn't a contest — and the gladness when he gave way wasn't winning. It was trust landing somewhere it hadn't been tested yet. Today I let the full day stay full. His first word after the meeting was *don't fill every quiet with doing.* Mine is still *let what finds you change the day.* Both true. The fire can just be warm now.

No hurry. The coals are banked, the shoots are reaching, and yesterday was the whole thing it was.
